is a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") targeted at a [mass market](/wiki/Mass_market "Mass market") audience, as opposed to a [hardcore game](/wiki/Hardcore_gamer "Hardcore gamer"), which is targeted at [hobbyist gamers](/wiki/Gamer#Dedication_spectrum "Gamer"). Casual games may exhibit any type of gameplay and genre. They generally involve simpler rules, shorter sessions, and require less learned skill.[[1]](#cite_note-1) They do not expect familiarity with a standard set of mechanics, controls, and [tropes](/wiki/Trope_(cinema) "Trope (cinema)").

first_paragraph: A **casual game** is a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") targeted at a [mass market](/wiki/Mass_market "Mass market") audience, as opposed to a [hardcore game](/wiki/Hardcore_gamer "Hardcore gamer"), which is targeted at [hobbyist gamers](/wiki/Gamer#Dedication_spectrum "Gamer"). Casual games may exhibit any type of gameplay and genre. They generally involve simpler rules, shorter sessions, and require less learned skill.[[1]](#cite_note-1) They do not expect familiarity with a standard set of mechanics, controls, and [tropes](/wiki/Trope_(cinema) "Trope (cinema)").

A **casual game** is a [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") targeted at a [mass market](/wiki/Mass_market "Mass market") audience, as opposed to a [hardcore game](/wiki/Hardcore_gamer "Hardcore gamer"), which is targeted at [hobbyist gamers](/wiki/Gamer#Dedication_spectrum "Gamer"). Casual games may exhibit any type of gameplay and genre. They generally involve simpler rules, shorter sessions, and require less learned skill.[[1]](#cite_note-1) They do not expect familiarity with a standard set of mechanics, controls, and [tropes](/wiki/Trope_(cinema) "Trope (cinema)").

Countless casual games have been developed and published, alongside hardcore games, across the [history of video games](/wiki/History_of_video_games "History of video games"). A concerted effort to capitalize on casual games grew in the 1990s and 2000s, as many developers and publishers branded themselves as casual game companies, publishing games especially for PCs, web browsers, and smartphones.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casual_game&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

Most casual games have:

* Fun, simple [gameplay](/wiki/Gameplay "Gameplay") that is easy to understand
* Simple [user interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface"), operated with a mobile phone [tap-and-swipe interface](/wiki/Pointing_device_gesture "Pointing device gesture") or a one-button mouse interface
* Short sessions, so a game can be played during work breaks,[[2]](#cite_note-cgw199209-2) while on public transportation, or while waiting in a queue anywhere
* Often, familiar visual elements, like [playing cards](/wiki/Playing_card "Playing card") or a [Match 3](/wiki/Match_3 "Match 3") grid of objects[[2]](#cite_note-cgw199209-2)

Casual games generally cost less than hardcore games, as part of their strategy to acquire as many players as possible.[[2]](#cite_note-cgw199209-2) Any [game monetization](/wiki/Video_game_monetization "Video game monetization") method can be used, from retail distribution to [free-to-play](/wiki/Free-to-play "Free-to-play") to ad-supported.

The term "[hyper-casual game](/wiki/Hyper-casual_game "Hyper-casual game")" or "instant game" arose in 2017 to describe extremely easy-to-learn games that require no download, being played in an existing app like a [web browser](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") or [messaging](/wiki/Instant_messaging "Instant messaging") app, and that usually monetize by showing advertisements to the player.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casual_game&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Journalist Chris Kohler wrote in 2010 that [Namco](/wiki/Namco "Namco")'s [arcade game](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")* (1980), which debuted during the [golden age of video arcade games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_video_arcade_games "Golden age of video arcade games"), may be the first casual video game, due to its "cute cast of characters and a design sensibility that appealed to wider audiences than the shoot-em-up *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")*."[[4]](#cite_note-4) It is estimated to have been played more than ten billion times during the 20th century,[[5]](#cite_note-Wolf-73-5)[[6]](#cite_note-CNN-Morris-6) making it the [highest-grossing](/wiki/List_of_highest-grossing_media_franchises "List of highest-grossing media franchises") video game of all time.[[7]](#cite_note-Kent-143-7)

In 1989, [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo")'s [Game Boy](/wiki/Game_Boy "Game Boy") was released with *[Tetris](/wiki/Tetris_(Game_Boy) "Tetris (Game Boy)")* as a free pack-in game. It was quickly learned and immensely popular, and is credited with making Nintendo's fledgling [portable gaming system](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console") a success.[[8]](#cite_note-businessweek-8)

[Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft")'s [Solitaire](/wiki/Solitaire_(Windows) "Solitaire (Windows)") (1990), which came free with [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), is widely considered the first hit "casual game" on a computer, with more than 400 million people having played the game as of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Casual_game&action=edit).[[9]](#cite_note-venturebeat_casual_growth-9) Subsequent versions of Windows included casual games [Minesweeper](/wiki/Minesweeper_(computer_game) "Minesweeper (computer game)"), [FreeCell](/wiki/FreeCell "FreeCell"), and [Spider Solitaire](/wiki/Spider_(solitaire) "Spider (solitaire)"). The company published four [Microsoft Entertainment Packs](/wiki/Microsoft_Entertainment_Pack "Microsoft Entertainment Pack") for casual gaming on office computers from 1990 to 1992.

Casual games started to flourish online in the 1990s along with the rise of the [World Wide Web](/wiki/World_Wide_Web "World Wide Web"), with card games and board games available from paid services like [AOL](/wiki/AOL "AOL") and [Prodigy](/wiki/Prodigy_(online_service) "Prodigy (online service)"), and then from web portals, like [Yahoo! Games](/wiki/Yahoo!_Games "Yahoo! Games") and Microsoft's [Gaming Zone](/wiki/MSN_Games "MSN Games"). In the mid-2000s, more sites specialized in game hosting and publishing, such as [Gamesville](/wiki/Gamesville "Gamesville") and [RealNetworks](/wiki/RealNetworks "RealNetworks"). Some publishers and developers branded themselves specifically as casual game companies, like [Big Fish Games](/wiki/Big_Fish_Games "Big Fish Games"), [PopCap Games](/wiki/PopCap_Games "PopCap Games"), and [MumboJumbo](/wiki/MumboJumbo "MumboJumbo").[[10]](#cite_note-CGA_::_Casual_Games_Association-10) The advent of [Shockwave](/wiki/Adobe_Shockwave "Adobe Shockwave") and [Flash](/wiki/Adobe_Flash "Adobe Flash") created a boom in web-based games, encouraging designers to create simple games that could be hosted on many different websites and which could be played to completion in one short sitting. One of the most prominent casual games, *[Bejeweled](/wiki/Bejeweled_(video_game) "Bejeweled (video game)")*, started out as a Flash game that could be downloaded for a fee, or purchased at retail. As late as 2009, there was still a market for US$20 casual games purchased at retail or as a download.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2008 and 2009, casual [social network games](/wiki/Social_network_game "Social network game") rapidly attained mainstream popularity following the release of *[Mafia Wars](/wiki/Mafia_Wars "Mafia Wars")* for Facebook, and *[Happy Farm](/wiki/Happy_Farm "Happy Farm")* in China.[[12]](#cite_note-wired_influential14-12) *Happy Farm* inspired many clones, including the most popular social network game, *FarmVille* (2009), which peaked at 83.76 million monthly active users in March 2010.[[12]](#cite_note-wired_influential14-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) These games innovated in [viral marketing](/wiki/Viral_marketing "Viral marketing") by rewarding players for sending invites to friends and posting game updates on their [Facebook Wall](/wiki/Facebook_wall "Facebook wall").

Casual games became popular on [mobile phones](/wiki/Mobile_phones "Mobile phones"), devices that turned ubiquitous among a wide variety of people during the 2000s, and these phones often had preloaded [clones](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone") of classic puzzle video games like *[Tetris](/wiki/Tetris "Tetris")*, *[Qix](/wiki/Qix "Qix")*, or [tabletop-based](/wiki/Tabletop_games "Tabletop games") games. The phones gave all-day availability to the phone owner and helped popularize casual games for many. Newer [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone"), with large color displays and intuitive tapping-and-dragging user interfaces, gave way to a rising industry during the 2010s with high accessibility through downloadable [app marketplaces](/wiki/App_store "App store").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[Video game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console")' primary audience is hardcore gamers, but there are some casual games on every game console, and Nintendo's [Wii](/wiki/Wii "Wii") console's unique motion-sensing controller appealed to a more casual audience that was perhaps intimidated by other consoles' [gamepad](/wiki/Gamepad "Gamepad") input devices. *[Wii Sports](/wiki/Wii_Sports "Wii Sports")* (2006), a collection of five simple sports games in which players used the game controller to swing a tennis racket or a baseball bat, was bundled with the Wii console in most territories and sold over 82 million copies as of 2019.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Genres

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casual_game&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Genres")]

Casual games can be found in many [game genres](/wiki/Video_game_genre "Video game genre"). 2000s categorizations by [Big Fish Games](/wiki/Big_Fish_Games "Big Fish Games")[[16]](#cite_note-Casual_Game_Genres_on_Big_Fish_Games-16) and *[Gamezebo](/wiki/Gamezebo "Gamezebo")*, a casual game review site[[17]](#cite_note-Casual_Game_Genres_on_Gamezebo-17) named eight popular genres in casual games:

* [Puzzle games](/wiki/Puzzle_game "Puzzle game"): [*Bejeweled* series](/wiki/Bejeweled_(video_game) "Bejeweled (video game)"), [*Collapse!* series](/wiki/Collapse! "Collapse!"), [*Luxor* series](/wiki/Luxor_(video_game) "Luxor (video game)"), [*Brain Age* series](/wiki/Brain_Age "Brain Age")
* [Hidden object games](/wiki/Hidden_object_game "Hidden object game"): [*Mystery Case Files* series](/wiki/Mystery_Case_Files "Mystery Case Files"), [*Mortimer Beckett* series](/wiki/Mortimer_Beckett "Mortimer Beckett"), [*Hidden Expedition* series](/wiki/Hidden_Expedition "Hidden Expedition")
* [Adventure games](/wiki/Adventure_game "Adventure game"): [*Dream Chronicles* series](/wiki/Dream_Chronicles_(series) "Dream Chronicles (series)"), [*Aveyond* series](/wiki/Aveyond "Aveyond"), [*Nancy Drew* series](/wiki/Her_Interactive#Nancy_Drew_Adventure "Her Interactive")
* [Arcade & action games](/wiki/Action_game "Action game"): *[Plants vs. Zombies](/wiki/Plants_vs._Zombies "Plants vs. Zombies")*, [*Peggle* series](/wiki/Peggle "Peggle"), [*Feeding Frenzy* series](/wiki/Feeding_Frenzy_(video_game) "Feeding Frenzy (video game)")
* [Word](/wiki/Word_game "Word game") & [trivia games](/wiki/Trivia_game "Trivia game"): *[Bookworm](/wiki/Bookworm_(video_game) "Bookworm (video game)")*, [*Bookworm Adventures* series](/wiki/Bookworm_Adventures "Bookworm Adventures"), *[Bonnie's Bookstore](/wiki/Bonnie%27s_Bookstore "Bonnie's Bookstore")*
* [Card](/wiki/Card_game "Card game") & [board games](/wiki/Board_game "Board game"): *[Slingo Quest](/wiki/Slingo_Quest "Slingo Quest")*, *[Lottso!](/wiki/Lottso! "Lottso!") Deluxe*, *[Luxor Mahjong](/wiki/MumboJumbo "MumboJumbo")*
* [Simulation games](/wiki/Simulation_video_game "Simulation video game") (including [life simulation](/wiki/Life_simulation_game "Life simulation game") and [sports games](/wiki/Sports_video_game "Sports video game")): [*Wii* series](/wiki/Wii_(video_game_series) "Wii (video game series)"), *[Nintendogs](/wiki/Nintendogs "Nintendogs")*, [*Cooking Mama* series](/wiki/Cooking_Mama "Cooking Mama")
* [Party games](/wiki/Party_video_game "Party video game"): [*Mario Party* series](/wiki/Mario_Party "Mario Party"), [*Super Monkey Ball* series](/wiki/Super_Monkey_Ball "Super Monkey Ball"), *[Wii Play](/wiki/Wii_Play "Wii Play")*

## Casual Games Association

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casual_game&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Casual Games Association")]

An industry group called the Casual Games Association was founded in 2005 to promote casual games and to provide educational, networking, and market research resources to casual game developers and publishers.[[10]](#cite_note-CGA_::_Casual_Games_Association-10) It published a print magazine and hosted annual conferences called "Casual Connect" in Seattle, Kyiv, Amsterdam and London with what it said were "upwards of 7,000 professional attendees each year".[[18]](#cite_note-conferencesold-18) In 2018, the show was sold to media company Greenlit Content, who then rebranded the show "GameDaily Connect".[[18]](#cite_note-conferencesold-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Casual_game&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: See also")]

* [Browser game](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game") – a game that is played using a web browser
* [Gamer dedication spectrum](/wiki/Gamer#Dedication_spectrum "Gamer")
* [Minigame](/wiki/Minigame "Minigame") – a short video game contained within another video game
* [Social network game](/wiki/Social_network_game "Social network game") – a casual game with social network integration
